title_en: I Tested a Decades-Old Premium Mechanical Keyboard, and It Proves Just How Unrecognizable the Mainstream Gaming Hardware Industry Has Become
summary_en: From scratchy MX Clears to aluminum gasket mounts and Hall effect switches—this is what happens when you compare a decade-old keyboard to a modern 2026 powerhouse.
body_en:
I've been into mechanical keyboards for the best part of a decade, long before they hit the mainstream. Long before we saw the mechanical keyboard hobby explode around the time of the pandemic and before mainstream manufacturers such as Asus, Corsair and others started trying to cater to the more premium end of the market.
Back then, things looked very different. It wasn't so much a feature list, or one specific selling point. Rather, it was the idea that a keyboard could be built around getting one thing right: a switch that felt genuinely good under your fingers, in a case solid enough to disappear under your hands and stay out of the way. A good keyboard was one you didn't necessarily notice, if that makes sense.
As much as that's still what I want from a keyboard in some respects, the market has moved on, and it's interesting to look at how good we thought we had it with a keyboard from the time, and how we've moved on in a few short years.
Sat in front of me is a Code V3B, a full-size premium choice that released in 2019 from the now-defunct WASD Keyboards in the USA – however, outside of a few small changes such as to a USB-C connector on the underside and the move to a small amount of software-driven customisation, it isn't too different to the old V2 model I remember wanting a lot when I was fourteen.
It hits the right notes in terms of what people wanted out of an enthusiast-grade mechanical keyboard at the time. The first thing that's immediately noticeable is that the chassis is entirely plastic – not a cheap-feeling plastic, but one with a pleasant textured finish with a reassuring amount of heft. This isn't a keyboard that's going to move too much when you plonk it down on your desk, that's for sure.
What might seem a silly thing to pick up on is the fact it's a full-size keyboard, too. As much as folks were opting for smaller layouts such as 60% or TKL, I don't remember it being anywhere near as common. You get a full complement of keys with the V3B, including a function row, nav cluster, and number pad. These days, the trend is to go smaller, for reclaiming that precious desk space.
For me at least, what's inside doesn't get enough credit, even today. The Code V3B remains one of the only keyboards you could purchase that came with funny MX switch variants – Red, Black, Brown and Blue were all too common, and it was near-impossible to get something with MX Clears or MX Greens as a pre-built keyboard apart from this one. Remember, this is when hot-swappable products were in their infancy, which left this Code product as one of the only ways I could get to try MX Clears.
For those unaware, MX Clears are a heavier version of MX Browns, with 65g of force needed to actuate, as opposed to the Brown’s slightly lighter 55cN. They offer more pronounced tactility, with more of a pronounced bump that makes them the perfect choice for typists in my book. I can get up to speed with them very briskly, and it's a satisfying, if heavier, keypress. Clears never really broke through in the same way the other MX switches did at the time, partially due to their rarity. After all, gaming culture drifted toward linear switches such as MX Reds because tactile bumps were seen as friction in fast inputs; office culture drifted toward something quieter than Clears' comparatively firm actuation with MX Browns. Even today, I remain convinced they're some of my favourite tactile switches, and using them in the Code V3B just reminds me of that.
However, this is where the Code V3B begins to show its age, most noticeably with the default keycaps it comes with. They're ABS rather than the more durable PBT, and after years of use they've gone shiny in exactly the way ABS always does — the same way cheap plastic keycaps do, the same way certain phone cases do, a slow polish from finger oils that no amount of cleaning fully reverses. It's not a flaw exactly, more a tell: ABS was simply what you got, unless you went out of the way to source something better. You could buy standalone PBT keycap sets then, as you can now, or get a keyboard with them on by default, but it wasn't all that common.
The cheaper-feeling keycaps also contribute to the disappointing acoustics of this product by modern standards. Sound isn't something that was particularly high on anyone's shopping list at the time, I don't think, as the Code V3B demonstrates. The overall profile is a bit thin, and there's a distinct ping on the stabilisers that's hard not to notice once you know to listen for it. Likewise, the switches aren't lubricated in any way, nor is there any sound damping in the case, hence the ping and a certain scratchiness to this keyboard's acoustics.
What's actually interesting about the V3B, looking back, is how little else it does. There is lighting, but it's purely white, and not really that configurable unless you want to do some firmware tinkering. Connectivity – sure, it's got some, but that's just a detachable cable for purely wired use. At the time, though, I don't remember thinking of these as shortcomings – it was arguably the entire point of the keyboard. Premium meant getting the fundamental job right and stopping there, which is where things have drastically shifted in the intervening period.
"The Makr Pro 75 does plenty right, but it doesn't quite stand amongst the pantheon of all-time mechanical keyboard designs. It's a super board for gaming and typing out of the box, and it offers good customization, but there are cheaper keyboards that offer better typing and similar features – so you have to really value Corsair's intuitive software and wider ecosystem for it to make sense."
For the modern 'premium' candidate, the Corsair MAKR Pro 75 seems like a nice middle ground. You could look up the price ladder to something from a modern, boutique manufacturer or even go down to the £100/$100 or so price to see how far we've come, but this Corsair option to me is a good indication of how the market's appetite has changed and what's deemed to be important in the mainstream.
Corsair's MAKR line of products is its intersection of enthusiast-grade features for a more gaming-oriented crowd, and this Pro model supplements the existing innovations with Hall effect switches, but more on those later.
As its name suggests, this Corsair product sizes the layout down to a 75% choice, eschewing the number pad and some navigation cluster keys to provide more desk space without sacrificing entirely on functionality. It's one of the clearest tells of the modern keyboard crowd, shifting from a fully functional arrangement to something that does exactly what people need while maintaining valuable desk space that's quite frankly a premium in 2026.
We've moved up in the world with doubleshot PBT keycaps out of the box, too, rather than the wretched ABS. This means they're made out of two separate pieces of plastic that are moulded together to mean legends that'll never fade or wear off, plus they are much more resistant to shine than ABS – I've never really seen PBT keycaps shine, even over extended use, although your mileage may vary, of course.
The chassis of the MAKR Pro 75 is aluminium, lending it to have a heavy-duty and durable feel. While it comes at the expense of some portability and lightness for a smaller form-factor keyboard, it's one of the first rather noticeable elements if you're coming from a near-decade-old keyboard with a plastic chassis in my experience.
Rather than being plate-mounted, the MAKR Pro 75 is also gasket-mounted, another one of the key changes the modern enthusiast's appetite has moved into mainstream territory. It lends a bouncier typing feel under the finger, which can feel more responsive than the increasingly rigid feel that the Code V3B has in conjunction with its heavier tactile switches.
Corsair has also put eight layers of internal sound dampening inside this keyboard, proving the shift in importance of acoustics, which directly addresses the issue of potentially iffy sound and the satisfaction that goes with a keyboard that sounds right. You also get infinitely more configurability with software, plus additional swappable modules for control dials, an LCD screen and even one that adds wireless connectivity for added versatility.
You also don't have a fixed choice of switches anymore, depending on the version of this Corsair keyboard you go for. More 'standard' variants opt for Corsair's own clone MX switches, which can be swapped in and out for other MX-style 3- or 5-pin switches without the need for de-soldering old ones and soldering new ones onto the PCB, as you'd have to do with the V3B. This Pro version features the brand's MGX Hyperdrive Hall effect switches, which are a different beast to conventional mechanical switches.
The benefits of Hall effect switches for gaming are well-trodden by this point, and it isn't necessarily a 'new' innovation. Hall effect switches have technically been around as long as conventional switches have, but we've only seen them used in the mainstream within the last few years, as people realised the benefit of non-fixed actuation.
Normal switches have a fixed point at which an input is registered due to the way the switches work. Those that utilise Hall effect sensors essentially work in a different manner and don't have a fixed point at which they actuate, unlocking benefits such as adjustable actuation points, rapid trigger and all the rest of it – essentially all of the tech you'll find in many of the best gaming keyboards out there.
None of these upgrades is small, nor isolated – each one answers a gripe that was had with the premium keyboards of old, checking off build quality, versatility and gaming performance, among other things. It proves that manufacturers feel folks want more of a complete solution in one product, rather than a keyboard that simply feels good under the fingers.
The MAKR Pro 75 didn't invent any of the features that make it feel modern. Instead, it's a useful case study in where the mechanical keyboard market has ended up. Things such as hot-swappable PCBs, PBT keycaps, gasket mounting, and better acoustics were more common in enthusiast circles, but have slowly but surely made their way into the mainstream as the mechanical keyboard hobby moved from a very niche hobby to something much more popular, so the more discerning customer became the target audience even for a more mainstream product from a bigger brand such as Corsair.
This is an intriguing product because it charts the development of both the hobbyist side and the gaming side of the mechanical keyboard sector. On the side of enthusiasts, they became more demanding as we got more choice in terms of switches and more avenues for customisation and personalisation opened up. The last few years have been a watershed moment for mechanical keyboard education, which has spilt over into the mainstream, leading even the big brands to take notice and make what I'd wager are more complete products from the get-go.
At the same time, gamers became more demanding too. Hall Effect switches accelerated that shift by offering genuinely new functionality rather than simply refining existing mechanical designs. They can be as brisk and responsive as the user demands, with features such as adjustable actuation, rapid trigger, and SOCD tech. This comes in conjunction with further innovations such as faster scan and polling rates and super low-latency wireless connections to provide speed and freedom without much compromise. Gone are the days when a gaming keyboard was simply a mechanical 'board with some RGB on it.
In many ways, that's where this story began. Pro players of games such as Starcraft 2 some fifteen years ago used niche mechanical products from brands such as Filco because they were more reliable and tactile than the comparable rubber domes, above all, although even then, they lacked the backlighting, game-state integration, and programmable keys a couple more years of refinement would yield.
Now, those same mechanical keyboards became the benchmark for a quality item, and the market has innovated further to bring Hall effect, TMR, and whatever is next to a gaming keyboard so players can be even faster with even more precise inputs than before.
That now comes with the added benefits of enthusiast-grade innovations mentioned above, so it can all be in one keyboard with a spec sheet as long as your arm. The checklist has grown longer because the market itself has become more discerning, and products like the MAKR Pro 75 are the result.
Looking back at a keyboard like the Code V3B, it's striking how focused the definition of 'premium' once was. A solid plastic chassis, reliable switches and functional looks were more than enough to justify a high price tag. There wasn't an expectation that it would be endlessly configurable, wirelessly connected or packed with enthusiast-grade refinements. It just had to excel at the fundamentals.
Fast forward to 2026, and that checklist has grown dramatically. A keyboard like the MAKR Pro 75 isn't trying to reinvent what a mechanical keyboard is, but it does reflect everything the market has gradually decided belongs in a premium product. Better materials, improved acoustics, hot-swappable hardware, Hall effect technology, modular accessories and extensive software customisation all exist because buyers have steadily become more discerning about what they're willing to pay for, and that's a good thing. Competition and innovation are always a good thing in this sector.
That's perhaps the biggest takeaway from looking at these two products side by side. Neither represents the "correct" approach to building a mechanical keyboard. Instead, they're snapshots of two very different moments in the hobby's evolution. The Code V3B belongs to an era when enthusiasts were primarily chasing typing feel and build quality, while the MAKR Pro 75 arrives in a market where gaming performance, flexibility and enthusiast-grade construction are designed to co-exist.
I still find myself yearning for the keyboard hobby of old, when the conversation felt a little more focused and getting the fundamentals right was enough to stand out. But it's equally clear that those days aren't coming back, nor should they necessarily. The growth of the hobby has broadened expectations, driven genuine innovation, and made premium keyboards better in many tangible ways. The definition of "premium" may have changed, but that's arguably only because the people buying these products have changed too – and products like the MAKR Pro 75 are simply the latest reflection of where that journey has led.
